coredata.dc:description: Meropenem, a broad-spectrum carbapenem antibiotic, is widely used in the treatment of severe bacterial infections. However, the accurate determination of meropenem in complex biological and environmental matrices remains a significant analytical challenge. In this study, we report the development of a highly sensitive and selective fluorescence sensor for the determination of meropenem using nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots capped with a surface molecular imprinting polymer (N-GQDs@MIP). Characterization and optical properties of the N-GQDs@MIP nanocomposite were thoroughly investigated using various analytical techniques. Upon excitation at 365 nm, the sensor exhibited strong fluorescence emission at 450 nm which quenched upon meropenem binding. Mechanistic studies revealed a static quenching process driven by strong host-guest interactions between meropenem and the imprinted polymer recognition sites, as confirmed by Stern-Volmer analysis and thermodynamic calculations. Density functional theory calculations also revealed specific molecular interactions between meropenem and the N-GQDs@MIP. Central composite design optimization was employed to systematically investigate the effect of key experimental parameters, such as pH, reaction time, and N-GQDs@MIP concentration, on the sensor's performance. A significant quadratic regression model was obtained, with a high coefficient of determination (R2 = 0.956) and excellent predictive capability that was further employed to maximize the sensor's sensitivity. Validation of the analytical method demonstrated a wide linear range of 25-1500 ng/mL, a low limit of detection of 7.42 ng/mL, and excellent selectivity against structurally similar antibiotics as well as good stability and reusability. The developed N-GQDs@MIP sensor was successfully applied to the determination of meropenem in pharmaceutical formulations, spiked human serum, urine, and environmental water samples with satisfactory recoveries and precision. Hence, this facile and robust fluorescence sensor offers a promising analytical platform for the sensitive, selective, and reliable quantification of meropenem in various clinical and environmental applications posing the current approach as a valuable alternative to existing analytical methods.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis process for N-GQDs@MIP: hydrothermal synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs), molecular imprinting of meropenem via sol-gel polymerization with APTES and TEOS, and template removal to create selective binding cavities.

Characterization of N-GQDs and N-GQDs@MIP: (A) Dynamic light scattering (DLS) size distribution analysis of N-GQDs showing a narrow particle size distribution with average hydrodynamic diameter of 2.97 ± 0.63 nm, indicating successful synthesis of quantum-sized nanoparticles. (B) DLS analysis of N-GQDs@MIP revealing increased particle size (105.71 ± 11.55 nm) after MIP coating, confirming successful polymer encapsulation of the N-GQDs core. (C) Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of N-GQDs displaying well-dispersed spherical nanoparticles with uniform size distribution around 3 nm, corroborating the DLS measurements. (D) TEM image of N-GQDs@MIP exhibiting distinct core-shell morphology where the darker N-GQDs core is surrounded by a lighter contrast MIP shell layer, demonstrating successful surface modification and molecular imprinting.

Optical properties of N-GQDs and N-GQDs@MIP: (A) UV–vis absorption spectra showing blue shift after MIP coating, (B) Excitation-dependent emission spectra of N-GQDs@MIP, (C) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectrum of N-GQDs@MIP at λex/λem = 365/450 nm, and (D) Fluorescence spectra before (a) and after (b) template removal.
DFT-calculated HOMO-LUMO energy levels of meropenem (−4.45 eV) and N-GQDs@MIP (−4.64 eV), illustrating the electron transfer mechanism responsible for fluorescence quenching.

Investigation of the fluorescence quenching mechanism between N-GQDs@MIP and meropenem. (A) Stern-Volmer plots (F0/F vs [meropenem]) at three different temperatures (298, 303, and 308 K), showing decreasing quenching constants with increasing temperature, characteristic of static quenching. (B) Schematic illustration of the static quenching mechanism, depicting the formation of a non-fluorescent ground-state complex between meropenem and the recognition cavities of N-GQDs@MIP. (C) Modified Stern-Volmer plots (F0/(F0–F) vs 1/[meropenem]) at different temperatures used to calculate association constants (Ka). (D) Van't Hoff plot (ln Ka vs 1/T) used to determine thermodynamic parameters (ΔH°, ΔS°, and ΔG°).
Analytical performance of N-GQDs@MIP sensor: (A) Fluorescence emission spectra at different meropenem concentrations, and (B) Calibration curve showing linear response range.
Thermodynamic parameters and binding constants for meropenem-N-GQDs@MIP interaction at different temperatures.

Statistical analysis using analysis of variance (ANOVA) to optimize the critical parameters of pH, N-GQDs@MIP concentration, and incubation time through response surface methodology using central composite design.
Method validation parameters were established according to ICH guidelines for the N-GQDs@MIP fluorescence sensor.

head.ce:title: A fluorescent sensor based on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots and molecularly imprinted polymers for selective and sensitive detection of meropenem in environmental and clinical matrices

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Meropenem, a broad-spectrum carbapenem antibiotic, is widely used in the treatment of severe bacterial infections. However, the accurate determination of meropenem in complex biological and environmental matrices remains a significant analytical challenge. In this study, we report the development of a highly sensitive and selective fluorescence sensor for the determination of meropenem using nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots capped with a surface molecular imprinting polymer (N-GQDs@MIP). Characterization and optical properties of the N-GQDs@MIP nanocomposite were thoroughly investigated using various analytical techniques. Upon excitation at 365 nm, the sensor exhibited strong fluorescence emission at 450 nm which quenched upon meropenem binding. Mechanistic studies revealed a static quenching process driven by strong host-guest interactions between meropenem and the imprinted polymer recognition sites, as confirmed by Stern-Volmer analysis and thermodynamic calculations. Density functional theory calculations also revealed specific molecular interactions between meropenem and the N-GQDs@MIP. Central composite design optimization was employed to systematically investigate the effect of key experimental parameters, such as pH, reaction time, and N-GQDs@MIP concentration, on the sensor's performance. A significant quadratic regression model was obtained, with a high coefficient of determination (R2 = 0.956) and excellent predictive capability that was further employed to maximize the sensor's sensitivity. Validation of the analytical method demonstrated a wide linear range of 25-1500 ng/mL, a low limit of detection of 7.42 ng/mL, and excellent selectivity against structurally similar antibiotics as well as good stability and reusability. The developed N-GQDs@MIP sensor was successfully applied to the determination of meropenem in pharmaceutical formulations, spiked human serum, urine, and environmental water samples with satisfactory recoveries and precision. Hence, this facile and robust fluorescence sensor offers a promising analytical platform for the sensitive, selective, and reliable quantification of meropenem in various clinical and environmental applications posing the current approach as a valuable alternative to existing analytical methods.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Antimicrobial resistance)AMR) has emerged as one of the most critical global health challenges of the 21st century, posing a serious threat to the medical advancements made in treating infectious diseases over the past decades [1–3]. The widespread use of antibiotics in human medicine, agriculture, and veterinary practices has accelerated the emergence of resistant bacterial strains, leading to increasingly ineffective conventional antibiotic treatments [4,5]. According to the World Health Organization (WHO), AMR could cause 10 million deaths annually by 2050, with an estimated global economic impact of $100 trillion [6]. This crisis extends beyond public health, imposing substantial economic burdens on healthcare systems worldwide. Effective monitoring of antimicrobial agents across various environments—from clinical settings to pharmaceutical waste and water systems—has become crucial for understanding and controlling resistance patterns [7,8]. While established analytical techniques such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) and mass spectrometry (MS) offer high accuracy, their costly and time-intensive nature, coupled with the need for sophisticated instrumentation, limits their widespread application [9,10]. This highlights an urgent need for developing simple, cost-effective, and rapid analytical methods for reliable antimicrobial agent detection and quantification to support global AMR surveillance efforts [11].

Among β-lactam antibiotics, meropenem—a broad-spectrum carbapenem—holds particular significance as a last-line therapy for severe infections caused by gram-negative pathogens [12]. Its stability against β-lactamase enzymes and rapid bactericidal activity make it indispensable for treating nosocomial pneumonia, sepsis, and meningitis in critical care settings [13]. However, up to 90 % of administered meropenem is excreted unmetabolized [14,15], entering wastewater systems through hospital effluents and agricultural runoff. Environmental monitoring studies have demonstrated that meropenem concentrations in hospital wastewater consistently exceed the minimal selective concentration of 30 μg/L, creating selective pressure for carbapenem-resistant bacteria development [16]. Hospital sewage contributes an average of 11.49 % of the microbial community in post-chlorinated wastewater treatment plant effluents, with meropenem-resistant populations surviving disinfection processes [17]. Environmental persistence of meropenem exerts selective pressure on microbial communities, promoting the horizontal transfer of carbapenems-encoding genes (e.g., blaKPC, blaNDM) to human pathogens [18]. Despite its clinical and environmental relevance, meropenem lacks robust detection methods capable of quantifying trace residues (μg/L–mg/L) in complex matrices such as biological fluids, pharmaceutical formulations, and wastewater [19]. Existing techniques, including HPLC and liquid chromatography–mass spectrometry (LC-MS), require costly instrumentation with limits of quantification ranging from 0.27 to 1.22 mg/L in biological matrices, laborious sample preparation, and specialized expertise, limiting their utility for sensitive environmental monitoring and high-throughput screening [20–23]. Immunoassays and electrochemical sensors, though simpler, often suffer from cross-reactivity with structurally similar β-lactams or instability in biological samples, underscoring the need for selective, sensitive, and cost-effective alternatives [24–26].

Quantum dots have emerged as powerful fluorescent probes in analytical chemistry due to their unique photophysical properties, including size-tunable emission, high quantum yield, exceptional photostability, and broad excitation spectra [27–29]. Among various quantum dot materials, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have garnered particular attention owing to their excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity, and cost-effective synthesis [30]. Nitrogen-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) further enhance these properties by introducing new energy levels and surface functionalities, resulting in improved quantum yield, enhanced electron transfer capabilities, and superior chemical stability [31]. The incorporation of nitrogen atoms into the graphene framework creates additional active sites for molecular interactions and enables tunable electronic properties [32,33]. However, despite these advantages, the direct application of N-GQDs as selective sensors remains challenging due to their limited molecular recognition capabilities [34]. The surface interactions between N-GQDs and target analytes often lack specificity, leading to potential interference from structurally similar compounds in complex matrices [35]. This limitation highlights the need for additional recognition elements to achieve the selectivity required for practical applications in analytical sensing.

Molecular imprinting technology has revolutionized the development of synthetic recognition elements by creating template-specific binding sites within polymer matrices [36]. These molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) exhibit antibody-like selectivity while offering superior stability, reproducibility, and cost-effectiveness compared to biological recognition elements [37–39]. The integration of MIPs with fluorescent nanomaterials has emerged as a promising strategy for developing highly selective sensors [40]. Recent studies have demonstrated successful coupling of MIPs with various quantum dots for detecting pharmaceuticals, environmental pollutants, and biomolecules [41,42]. The combination typically involves surface modification of quantum dots with functional monomers, followed by controlled polymerization in the presence of template molecules to create specific recognition cavities [43]. This approach has been successfully applied to various analytical targets, yielding sensors with excellent selectivity and sensitivity [44]. The synergistic combination of MIPs' selective recognition capabilities with the superior optical properties of quantum dots offers a powerful platform for developing advanced chemical sensors, addressing the limitations of traditional analytical methods while maintaining simplicity and cost-effectiveness [45].

In this work, we report the development of a facile and efficient fluorescence-based sensor for the determination of meropenem using N-doped graphene quantum dots capped with a molecularly imprinted polymer (N-GQDs@MIP). The characterization and optical properties of the N-GQDs@MIP composite are investigated in detail using various analytical techniques, including transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), dynamic light scattering (DLS), UV–Vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. The sensing mechanism of meropenem by the N-GQDs@MIP sensor is elucidated, using quantum mechanical calculations, Stern-Volmer analysis and thermodynamic studies. The performance of the sensor is optimized through a design of experiments approach, employing a central composite design to identify the critical parameters influencing the analytical response. The stability, reusability, adsorption kinetics, and selectivity of the N-GQDs@MIP sensor are also thoroughly evaluated, demonstrating its potential for practical applications in the determination of meropenem in complex matrices, such as pharmaceutical formulations, biological fluids, and environmental samples. The study provides valuable insights into the rational design of MIP-based fluorescent sensors for the sensitive and selective detection of pharmaceuticals, contributing to the advancement of analytical methodologies in the field of environmental and therapeutic drug monitoring as well as pharmaceutical quality control.

All chemicals used were of analytical grade. Citric acid monohydrate (≥99.0 %), ethylenediamine (≥99.5 %), 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES, 99 %), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, ≥99.0 %), and ammonium hydroxide solution (28–30 % NH3 basis) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Meropenem trihydrate (≥98 %) was obtained from the Egyptian Drug Authority (EDA), Cairo, Egypt. Methanol, acetonitrile and acetic acid (HPLC grade) were acquired from Fisher Scientific Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Distilled water was used throughout all experiments. Stock solutions of meropenem (100 μg/mL) were prepared in distilled water and stored at 4 °C. Working standards were prepared by appropriate dilution of the stock solution with distilled water.

Fluorescence measurements were performed using a Jasco FP-6200 spectrofluorometer equipped with a xenon lamp source, with excitation and emission slit widths set to 5 nm and scan rate (4000 nm/min). TEM images were obtained using a JEOL JEM-2100F microscope (Tokyo, Japan) operating at 200 kV. DLS measurements were conducted using a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern, UK). FTIR spectra were recorded on a Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS50 FTIR spectrometer (Waltham, MA, USA) in the range of 400–4000 cm−1. UV–visible absorption spectra were measured using a Shimadzu UV-1800 spectrophotometer (Kyoto, Japan). pH measurements were performed using a Jenway 3510 pH meter (Staffordshire, UK).

Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) were synthesized via a one-pot hydrothermal method as previously reported with [46] slight modifications. Briefly, citric acid monohydrate (2.1 g) and ethylenediamine (670 μL) were dissolved in 20 mL ultrapure water, and the solution was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. The autoclave was heated at 200 °C for 5 h and then cooled naturally to room temperature. The resulting brown solution was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 10 min to remove large particles, and the supernatant containing N-GQDs was collected. The supernatant was dried under vacuum at 60 °C for 24 h to obtain solid N-GQDs. The dried N-GQDs were then redispersed in ultrapure water to prepare a stock solution of 2.0 mg/mL and stored at 4 °C.

For the molecular imprinting step, the sol-gel polymerization procedure was adapted from literature methods [47,48] and optimized for meropenem detection. Five mL of N-GQDs solution (2.0 mg/mL) was mixed with 100 mg meropenem in 20 mL ethanol. After stirring for 30 min, 200 μL APTES and 200 μL TEOS were added dropwise, followed by the addition of 100 μL ammonium hydroxide solution. The mixture was stirred at room temperature for 12 h to complete the polymerization process. The resulting N-GQDs@MIP nanoparticles were collected by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 10 min and washed three times with methanol/acetic acid (9:1, v/v) to remove the template molecules. The meropenem removal efficiency was monitored by UV–Vis spectrophotometric analysis of the washing solutions at 298 nm, and template removal was considered complete when consecutive washing solutions showed no detectable meropenem (absorbance <0.01), achieving >98 % removal efficiency. Finally, the products were washed with ultrapure water until neutral pH and dried under vacuum at 40 °C for 24 h. A non-imprinted polymer (N-GQDs@NIP) was prepared following the same procedure but without adding the template molecule (meropenem) during the synthesis process. Characterization of the N-GQDs and N-GQDs@MIP was performed using TEM, FTIR, and DLS where as the optical properties were studied using fluorescence and UV–Vis spectroscopy.

The sensing conditions were optimized using a central composite design approach. Three key parameters—pH (4.0–10.0), N-GQDs@MIP concentration (100–800 μL), and incubation time (1–10 min)—were investigated, with fluorescence quenching efficiency (%) as the response variable. For each measurement, appropriate volumes of N-GQDs@MIP solution were mixed with meropenem standard solution (1.5 μg/mL) in phosphate buffer at different pH values. The mixture was incubated for various time periods at room temperature before measuring the fluorescence intensity at λex/λem = 365/450 nm. The optimal conditions derived from ANOVA (p < 0.0001) were pH 7.8, N-GQDs@MIP volume of 760 μL, and an incubation time of 7 min.

The method was validated in line with ICH Q2(R1) guidelines, assessing key parameters such as linearity, sensitivity, accuracy, precision, robustness, and selectivity. Calibration curves were prepared using meropenem standard solutions spanning from 25 to 1500 ng/mL. The regression equation and correlation coefficient (r2) were determined. The limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) were determined by using the standard deviation of the regression line and the slope of the calibration curve. Accuracy was verified through recovery experiments at three different concentrations (75, 750, and 1000 ng/mL), each analyzed three times. Precision was evaluated by conducting both intraday (repeatability) and interday (intermediate precision) studies. Robustness was examined by making minor adjustments to experimental conditions, such as varying the pH by ±0.2, the volume of N-GQDs@MIP by ±10 μL, and the incubation time by ±1 min. Finally, selectivity was assessed by analyzing the method's response in the presence of potential interfering substances at 10-fold excess, including antibiotics with similar structures (e.g., imipenem, ertapenem), ceftazidime and ciprofloxacin, as well as common pharmaceutical excipients like starch and lactose, and common plasma components such as tryptophan and albumin.

The validated fluorescence-based method was applied to the determination of meropenem in pharmaceutical formulations and spiked samples of plasma, urine, waste hospital water and tap water. For the pharmaceutical samples, 10 mg meropenem powder for injection was dissolved in deionized water and diluted to the appropriate concentration range. The content of meropenem was determined using the developed method and statistically compared to the results obtained by a reported HPLC method.

The developed N-GQDs@MIP fluorescence method was applied to the determination of meropenem in biological samples (plasma and urine) and environmental samples (hospital wastewater and tap water). To ensure efficient extraction of meropenem from complex matrices, the salting-out assisted liquid-liquid extraction (SALLE) method was employed for all sample types. For each sample type, four sets were prepared by spiking with meropenem to achieve initial concentrations of 500, 1000, 5000, and 7500 ng/mL. To 1 mL of each spiked sample (plasma, urine, hospital wastewater, or tap water), 1 mL of acetonitrile and 250 mg of sodium chloride (NaCl) were added to induce phase separation. The mixture was vortexed for 2 min and centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 10 min. The organic phase (upper layer) was collected, dried under a gentle nitrogen stream at 40 °C, and reconstituted in 1 mL of phosphate buffer (pH 7.8). The solution was then mixed with 760 μL of N-GQDs@MIP solution, incubated for 7 min at room temperature completed to 10 mL with distilled water, and analyzed under fluorescence conditions (λex/λem = 365/450 nm). A blank solution was prepared using the same procedure but without meropenem spiking. Each sample type was analyzed in triplicate to ensure statistical reliability. The recoveries and precision of the method were calculated to validate its accuracy and reproducibility in complex matrices.

N-GQDs were synthesized using a simple one-pot method with citric acid and ethylenediamine as precursors. Under hydrothermal conditions, the dehydration and carbonization process yielded fluorescent N-doped GQDs. The surface of the N-GQDs was then functionalized with a molecular imprinting polymer, employing APTES as the functional monomer, TEOS as the cross-linker, meropenem as the template molecule, and ammonia as the initiator in a sol-gel copolymerization process (Fig. 1 ). Finally, the template molecule was removed to generate the imprinted sites for the target analyte, meropenem and the N-GQDs@MIP were obtained. Upon excitation at 365 nm, the N-GQDs@MIP exhibited strong fluorescence emission at around 450 nm that could be used for the sensitive detection of meropenem.

The ratio of APTES to TEOS was a critical parameter that needed to be optimized to obtain an effective and selective MIP for meropenem detection. Different ratios were tried, and it was noted that a higher APTES to TEOS ratio led to stronger binding sites and better recognition of the template molecule until the ration became 1:1 (Fig. S1). Increasing the ratio above 1:1 led to decreased selectivity and binding sites resulting in a decrease in the quenching efficiency (%). This could be attributed to the formation of less ordered and more rigid MIP structure at higher TEOS concentrations that impedes target molecule diffusion and accessibility to the binding sites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and spectral characteristics of N-GQDs@MIP
ce:section.ce:para: The N-GQDs and the N-GQDs@MIP were characterized using various techniques to confirm the successful synthesis and investigate their physical and optical properties. The size of the N-GQDs and N-GQDs@MIP were studied using DLS, indicating average hydrodynamic diameters of 2.97 ± 0.63 nm (Fig. 2 A) and 105.71 ± 11.55 nm (Fig. 2B) respectively, confirming the formation of the MIP shell around the N-GQDs core. The TEM images showed the spherical morphology of the N-GQDs with an average size of around 3 nm (Fig. 2C), while the N-GQDs@MIP exhibited a larger size with a core-shell structure (Fig. 2D) which comes in line with the DLS findings. The FT-IR spectra were also recorded to identify the functional groups present in the N-GQDs and the N-GQDs@MIP. The FT-IR spectrum of the N-GQDs showed characteristic peaks at 3411 and 3160 cm−1, corresponding to O–H and N–H stretching vibrations, respectively (Fig. S2). Additional peaks at 1670 and 1613 cm−1 were attributed to CO and CC stretching (Fig. S2). In comparison, the N-GQDs@MIP showed additional peaks at 1100 and 978 cm−1, assigned to Si–O–Si and Si–O–C asymmetric stretching vibrations. Additionally, a bending vibration of the Si–O bond was also observed at 475 cm−1, indicating the successful grafting of the silica-based MIP onto the surface of the N-GQDs (Fig. S2). While XPS analysis would provide more definitive confirmation of silicon incorporation, the FTIR evidence clearly demonstrates the presence of silica-based functional groups from the APTES and TEOS precursors.

The optical properties of the N-GQDs and N-GQDs@MIP were further investigated using UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. The UV–vis absorption spectra of the N-GQDs showed a characteristic peak at 340 nm, associated with the n→π∗ transition of CO bonds (Fig. 3 A). This peak is typical for GQDs and confirms the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of the nanoparticles. Interestingly, a slight blue shift with a hypochromic effect of the absorption peak was observed for the N-GQDs@MIP (Fig. 3A). This spectral change can be attributed to the decrease in the conjugated structure of the GQDs after the coating of the MIP layer. The blue shift suggests a reduction in the extent of the π-electron system, likely due to the interaction between the N-GQDs and the silica-based MIP matrix. The hypochromic effect (decrease in absorption intensity) further supports this interpretation, as it indicates a decrease in the molar absorptivity of the chromophores in the N-GQDs@MIP system.

The fluorescence properties of the N-GQDs@MIP were comprehensively studied by varying the excitation wavelength from 320 to 380 nm. A clear excitation-dependent emission behavior was observed, which is a characteristic feature of GQDs (Fig. 3B). This phenomenon is often attributed to the presence of multiple emissive states or the size distribution of the quantum dots. When the N-GQDs@MIP were excited at 365 nm, a strong emission peak at around 450 nm was observed, indicating their potential for fluorescence-based detection applications (Fig. 3C). The blue emission (450 nm) suggests that the N-GQDs@MIP retain the excellent optical properties of the core N-GQDs, despite the MIP coating. Notably, upon template removal, a significant enhancement in the fluorescence intensity of the N-GQDs@MIP was observed (Fig. 3D). This enhancement can be explained by two factors: (1) the formation of selective recognition cavities in the MIP matrix, which reduces the quenching effect of the template molecules on the N-GQDs, and (2) the possible removal of residual unreacted monomers or oligomers during the template extraction process, which might have acted as quenchers. The increase in fluorescence intensity after template removal is crucial for the sensor's performance, as it provides a higher initial signal for subsequent analyte detection. To further investigate the fluorescence behavior, we performed a comparative analysis of the fluorescence quantum yield (QY) for N-GQDs and N-GQDs@MIP. The QY of N-GQDs@MIP (0.21) was found to be slightly lower than that of bare N-GQDs (0.28). This decrease could result from multiple factors including the encapsulation effect of the MIP layer, which may limit light access to the N-GQDs core and reduce radiative pathways, as well as potential energy transfer processes between the N-GQDs and the polymer matrix. Further spectroscopic studies would be needed to distinguish between these mechanisms. However, the relatively high QY of N-GQDs@MIP still indicates strong fluorescence properties, suitable for sensitive detection applications.

The fluorescence detection mechanism of meropenem by the N-GQDs@MIP was investigated using quantum mechanical calculations, Stern-Volmer analysis and thermodynamics studies. Previous literature suggested that the fluorescence quenching of surface imprinted quantum dots with different analytes occurs primarily due to different mechanisms, such as static quenching, dynamic quenching, or a combination of both. Other possible quenching mechanisms, such as energy transfer or electron transfer, were also considered. To elucidate the dominant quenching mechanism in the present system, we first performed quantum mechanical calculations to investigate the electronic interactions between meropenem and the N-GQDs@MIP. Hence, density functional theory (DFT) calculations were carried out using Gaussian 09 software to obtain the optimized geometries, molecular orbitals, and energy levels of meropenem and the N-GQDs@MIP system. B3LYP/6-31G(d) level of theory was employed to optimize the geometries and calculate the electronic states. Such a basis set is a good compromise between accuracy and computational cost; hence, it is widely used in quantum chemical studies. The calculated HOMO-LUMO energy gap of meropenem was −4.45 eV, which is lower than the −4.64 eV energy gap calculated for the N-GQDs@MIP (Fig. 4 ). This energetic alignment, with a smaller HOMO-LUMO gap for meropenem compared to the N-GQDs@MIP, indicates a favorable electron transfer process from the higher LUMO of N-GQDs@MIP (−0.0394 Hartree, orbital 101) to the lower LUMO of meropenem (−0.0724 Hartree, orbital 103), resulting in the observed fluorescence quenching (Fig. 4).

In addition, the electronic energy (E) for meropenem, N-GQDs@MIP and their binding complex were calculated based on the optimized structures. The changes in energy (ΔE) associated with the binding of meropenem to the N-GQDs@MIP were calculated according to the following equation:
Δ E = E
(
N − GQDs @ MIP − meropenem
)
− E
(
N − GQDs @ MIP
)
− E
( meropenem )
= − 0.0146
Hartee
This negative value indicates a favorable binding between meropenem and the N-GQDs@MIP, stabilized by non-covalent interactions such as hydrogen bonding and π-π stacking.

Stern-Volmer analysis was also performed to study the fluorescence quenching mechanism in detail according to the following equation:
F 0 / F = 1 +
K SV
[ Q ]

Here, F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of the quencher (meropenem), respectively, and KSV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant. The Stern-Volmer plot (Fig. 5 A) showed a linear relation that decreases upon increasing the temperature, suggesting a static quenching mechanism, where the quencher (meropenem) forms a non-fluorescent complex with the fluorophore (N-GQDs@MIP) in the ground state (Fig. 5B). The calculated KSV values at 298, 303, and 308 K were 1.03 × 106, 0.88 × 106, and 0.74 × 106 M−1, respectively (Table 1 ). The decrease in the Stern-Volmer quenching constant with increasing temperature is typical of static quenching, as the formation of the non-fluorescent complex is less favorable at higher temperatures. Furthermore, the association constant (Ka) was also calculated based on the modified Stern-Volmer equation (Fig. 5C):
F 0 /
(
F 0 − F
)
= 1 + 1 /
(
K a
[ Q ]
)
which gave values of 1.29 × 106, 1.17 × 106, and 1.06 × 106 M−1 at 298, 303, and 308 K, respectively (Table 1). The decrease in the association constant with increasing temperature confirms the static nature of the quenching process. Furthermore, the number of binding sites (n) was calculated to be approximately 0.904, indicating a 1:1 binding stoichiometry between meropenem and the N-GQDs@MIP based on the double logarithmic Stern-Volmer equation:
log
(
F 0 − F
)
/ F = log
K b
+ n
log
[ Q ]

A decrease in the number of binding sites with increasing temperature also supports the static quenching mechanism (Table 1), as higher temperatures disrupt the formation of the non-fluorescent complex.

Other thermodynamic parameters such as enthalpy, entropy and Gibb's free energy change were also calculated from the Van't Hoff plot (Fig. 5D) upon changing the temperature (Table 1). The negative ΔG° value of −34.88 kJ/mol at 298 K confirmed the spontaneous and favorable binding of meropenem to the imprinted cavities. The negative ΔH° of −14.63 kJ/mol indicated an exothermic binding process while the positive ΔS° of 67.90 J/mol·K suggested an increase in the randomness of the system upon complex formation. Although the increase in entropy may seem counterintuitive for a binding process, it can be attributed to the release of water molecules from the hydration shells of the interacting species, leading to an overall increase in the system's entropy.

To comprehensively differentiate between static and dynamic quenching mechanisms, the temperature dependence of the fluorescence quenching process was systematically analyzed. In dynamic quenching, molecular collisions between fluorophore and quencher increase with temperature due to enhanced diffusion rates, leading to higher quenching constants at elevated temperatures. Conversely, static quenching involves ground-state complex formation, which becomes thermodynamically less favorable at higher temperatures, resulting in decreased quenching constants. The experimental results clearly demonstrate the latter behavior, with Ksv values decreasing from 1.03 × 106 M−1 at 298 K to 0.74 × 106 M−1 at 308 K, definitively confirming static quenching as the dominant mechanism. The negative enthalpy change (ΔH° = −14.63 kJ/mol) provides additional evidence for ground-state complex formation, as dynamic quenching processes typically exhibit positive or near-zero enthalpy changes due to the absence of specific binding interactions. The 1:1 binding stoichiometry (n ≈ 0.904) further supports the formation of discrete complexes between meropenem and the imprinted cavities, which is characteristic of static quenching rather than the non-specific collisional interactions observed in dynamic quenching. Therefore, the combined quantum mechanical calculations, Stern-Volmer analysis, and thermodynamic studies conclusively demonstrate that the fluorescence quenching of N-GQDs@MIP by meropenem occurs primarily via a static quenching mechanism, where a non-fluorescent ground-state complex is formed between the analyte and the imprinted polymer-capped quantum dots. Another possible mechanism is electron transfer from the excited state of the N-GQDs to the LUMO of meropenem which could also contribute to the observed fluorescence quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the experimental conditions using central composite design
ce:section.ce:para:
To optimize the experimental parameters for the determination of meropenem, a central composite design was implemented as a response surface methodology. The independent variables considered were the pH of the solution, the concentration of the N-GQDs@MIP expressed as volume and the incubation time, while the dependent variable was the fluorescence quenching efficiency. A total of 20 experimental runs were performed to obtain the optimum conditions (Table S1). Analysis of the results indicated that a reduced quadratic model was the best fit to describe the relation between the variables and the response where the pH and the N-GQDs@MIP concentration had the most significant effects followed by incubation time. Interestingly, a quadratic effect was also observed for both the pH and the N-GQDs@MIP concentration, indicating the existence of an optimal value for these parameters. The quadratic equation that best described the data was:
QE % = 69.7542 + 11.9525
∗
A + 15.2261
∗
B + 3.82435
∗
C + 7.7421
∗
AB − 17.3265
∗
A 2
− 10.0688
∗
B 2
where A represents the pH, B represents the N-GQDs@MIP concentration and C represents the incubation time. Such design was able to model the non-linear relationship between the variables and the response and determine the optimum experimental conditions. Positive coefficient values for the linear terms indicate that increasing these variables results in a higher fluorescence quenching efficiency, while the negative coefficients for the quadratic terms suggest the existence of maximum values for these parameters. ANOVA analysis confirmed the statistical significance of the model (p < 0.0001) with an R2 value of 0.956, suggesting an excellent fit between the experimental data and the predicted values (Table 2 ). It also revealed that the pH and N-GQDs@MIP concentration were the most influential parameters affecting the fluorescence quenching with their interactions and quadratic effects also contributing significantly (p < 0.01). The lack of fit test was non-significant with p value = 0.868 (Table 2), confirming that the model adequately represents the relationship between the independent variables and the response.

Validation of the developed model was carried out through inspecting the residual plots, which showed normality of the residuals and absence of any trending patterns, confirming the adequacy of the model (Fig. S3). In addition, the actual vs predicted values showed excellent agreement, further confirming the validity of the optimized conditions (Fig. S4). Regarding the effect of run order, no trends were identified when examining the run vs residual plot, further supporting the model validity (Fig. S5). The leverage vs run order was also plotted to identify any influential data points and no issues were found (Fig. S6). Besides, the coefficient of variation (CV%) was low at 9.71 %, indicating good precision of the experimental data. Adequate precision was also obtained, with a value of 19.67 which is well above the recommended value of 4. Moreover, adjusted and predicted R2 values of 0.935 and 0.915 respectively, were close to each other and the experimental R2 value of 0.956, confirming the good predictive ability of the model.

A major advantage of the central composite design is that it allows the determination of the optimum values of the independent variables that maximize the response, in this case the fluorescence quenching efficiency. The pH and the N-GQDs@MIP concentration plots showed a maximum at around pH 7.5 and a N-GQDs@MIP concentration of 700 μL (Fig. S7A and S7B). Increasing or decreasing the pH concentration beyond these values resulted in a decrease in the fluorescence quenching efficiency. This could be attributed to the ionization state of the meropenem and the N-GQDs@MIP at different pH values affecting the binding interactions. Low pH values may have resulted in protonation of the carboxylic group of meropenem, reducing its ability to interact with the N-GQDs@MIP. On the other hand, high pH values could have led to the deprotonation of the amino group of the N-GQDs@MIP, also diminishing the binding. On the other hand, a lesser quadratic effect was observed for N-GQDs@MIP concentration, where increasing the volume beyond the optimum led to a gradual decrease in the response, likely due to reduced accessibility of the cavities for meropenem at higher polymer concentrations (Fig. S7B). The incubation time on the other hand, showed a linear effect where longer incubation resulted in higher QE% (Fig. S7C). 3D-response surface and plots were then generated to visualize the interactions between the parameters and determine the optimum conditions. In agreement with the ANOVA results, the 3D plot (Fig. S7D) revealed that the pH and N-GQDs@MIP concentration had an interactive and quadratic effect on the QE% where there existed an optimal value for each factor that maximized the response.

To optimize the experimental conditions for the determination of meropenem using the N-GQDs@MIP, desirability function analysis was employed. The objective was to maximize the quenching efficiency while maintaining the pH, N-GQDs@MIP and incubation time variables in range. The optimal conditions were determined to be pH 7.8, 760 μL of N-GQDs@MIP, and an incubation time of 7 min which achieved the highest fluorescence quenching and maximum binding efficiency (Fig. S8). These optimized parameters were subsequently used for the further characterization and analytical performance of the N-GQDs@MIP sensor for meropenem.

The stability and reusability of the N-GQDs@MIP sensor were investigated under optimized conditions. The stability of the sensor was evaluated by measuring its fluorescence intensity response every 30 min for 3 h. The results showed that the sensor maintained more than 95 % of its initial response over the 3-h period, indicating excellent stability (Fig. S9).

To assess the reusability, the N-GQDs@MIP sensor was subjected to repeated binding and elution cycles with meropenem solutions. Briefly, 760 μL of the sensor was incubated with a 1 μg/mL meropenem solution for 7 min under stirring at room temperature, then the fluorescence intensity was measured. The sensor was then regenerated by washing with methanol/acetic acid (9:1 v/v) to remove the bound meropenem, and the process was repeated for 10 cycles. Since the N-GQDs@MIP are large nanoparticles, they can be easily separated from the solution by simple centrifugation. The sensor retained over 94 % of its initial response even after the 10 consecutive binding/elution cycles, demonstrating its robust reusability for repeated use in sample analysis (Fig. S10).

To evaluate the imprinting efficiency, the binding capacity of the N-GQDs@MIP was compared to that of the non-imprinted N-GQDs@NIP. Under optimized conditions, the N-GQDs@MIP exhibited a significantly higher equilibrium adsorption capacity of 1.860 μg/mg, compared to only 0.345 μg/mg for the N-GQDs@NIP. This corresponds to an imprinting factor of 5.4. The substantial difference in adsorption capacity confirms the successful imprinting of the meropenem template and the high binding affinity of the imprinted cavities.

The accuracy of the method was evaluated according to ICH guidelines at three different concentration levels (75, 750, and 1000 ng/mL). The average accuracy was determined to be 99.11 ± 0.86 %, confirming the method's reliability (Table 3). Precision was further assessed through intraday and interday studies, with relative standard deviations (%RSD) of less than 0.87 % and 1.68 %, respectively. These results highlight the method's reproducibility and consistency over time.

Robustness tests were conducted by introducing deliberate variations in key experimental parameters, including pH, N-GQDs@MIP volume, and incubation time. The sensor demonstrated minimal variation in response, with recoveries of 99.21 ± 0.82 % for pH, 98.33 ± 0.89 % for N-GQDs@MIP volume, and 99.54 ± 1.14 % for incubation time (Table 3). These findings confirm the robustness of the method, ensuring its reliability under varying experimental conditions.

The selectivity of the N-GQDs@MIP sensor was evaluated by comparing the fluorescence responses of meropenem with several structurally similar antibiotics, including imipenem, ertapenem, ceftazidime, and ciprofloxacin. The sensor exhibited significantly higher quenching efficiency for meropenem, with minimal interference from other antibiotics, confirming its high selectivity (Fig. S11A). Additionally, the sensor's performance was tested in the presence of common pharmaceutical excipients, ions, and biological components such as starch, lactose, cellulose, Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2−, SO4 2−, PO4 3−, glucose, albumin, and amino acids. No significant interference was observed, further validating the sensor's selectivity and applicability in real-world samples (Fig. S11B).

The practical applicability of the developed N-GQDs@MIP sensor was thoroughly evaluated through statistical comparison with a reported method and analysis of various real-world samples. Statistical analysis comparing the developed method with the reported chromatographic method [22] showed excellent agreement, with mean recoveries of 99.64 ± 0.65 % and 99.43 ± 1.11 % respectively (Table 4 ). The calculated t-test value (0.366) and F-value (0.336) were well below their corresponding tabulated values (2.306 and 6.338) at P = 0.05, indicating no significant difference between the two methods (Table 4). Interval hypothesis testing revealed (θL = −1.118, θU = 1.539) fell within the acceptable range of ±2 %, further confirming the accuracy of the developed method.

The sensor's performance was evaluated across different matrices including biological fluids and environmental water samples (Table 5 ). In plasma samples, the method demonstrated excellent recoveries ranging from 98.01 % to 104.17 % across concentrations from 50 to 750 ng/mL, with relative standard deviations (RSDs) between 1.90 % and 3.43 %. Similarly, analysis of urine samples showed robust performance with recoveries between 95.56 % and 104.09 % and RSDs ranging from 1.23 % to 3.38 % (Table 5).

Environmental water analysis revealed the sensor's capability in handling complex matrices. Hospital wastewater analysis showed consistent recoveries from 95.86 % to 100.87 % across the tested concentration range, with RSDs not exceeding 3.28 %. The method also performed well with tap water samples, yielding recoveries between 95.40 % and 101.35 % with RSDs ranging from 0.56 % to 2.33 % (Table 4).

These results demonstrate the sensor's reliability and accuracy across various real-world matrices, making it suitable for routine monitoring of meropenem in both clinical and environmental applications. The high recoveries and low RSDs across different sample types validate the robustness and practical utility of the developed N-GQDs@MIP sensor.

The analytical performance of the developed N-GQDs@MIP fluorescence sensor was evaluated against various reported methods for meropenem determination in the literature. Chromatographic techniques, particularly LC-MS/MS methods, have been widely employed for meropenem analysis due to their high sensitivity and selectivity. For instance, Huang et al. developed an LC-MS/MS method achieving a wide linear range of 50-25,000 ng/mL with an LOD of 15 ng/mL in bacterial media analysis [20]. While this method offered superior detection capabilities, it required sophisticated instrumentation and extensive method optimization for different matrices. Similarly, Roth et al. reported an HPLC-UV method with a linear range of 3500–200,000 ng/mL but suffered from matrix effects and limited sensitivity at lower concentrations [21]. Kipper et al. proposed a rapid LC-UV method with an LOQ of 100 ng/mL, yet faced challenges with mobile phase optimization and peak resolution [22]. These chromatographic methods, while powerful, generally demand expensive instrumentation, high maintenance costs, and specialized expertise that limit their accessibility for routine analysis.

Alternative approaches have been explored to address the limitations of chromatographic methods. Microbiological assays, such as the cylinder-plate method reported by Mendez et al., offered a simple and cost-effective approach with a linear range of 1500–6000 ng/mL [24]. However, these biological methods suffered from several drawbacks including lengthy analysis times (24–48 h), poor reproducibility due to variations in bacterial growth, and significant interference from other antibiotics present in the sample. Electrochemical methods have also been investigated, with Atta et al. developing a carbon nanotube-modified electrode achieving a linear range of 115-26,900 ng/mL and an LOD of 57.5 ng/mL [25]. Similarly, Attia et al. reported a voltametric method capable of simultaneous detection of multiple drugs, including meropenem, with an LOD of 76.5 ng/mL [26]. While these electrochemical approaches offered rapid response times and reasonable sensitivity, they often faced challenges related to surface fouling, poor electrode reproducibility, limited linear range, and significant interference from electroactive species commonly present in biological samples.

The developed N-GQDs@MIP fluorescence sensor demonstrates several significant advantages over existing methods. The sensor achieves excellent sensitivity with an LOD of 7.42 ng/mL and a practical linear range of 25-1500 ng/mL, making it suitable for various clinical and environmental applications. The molecular imprinting approach provides superior selectivity against structurally similar antibiotics, addressing a common limitation of conventional methods. Notably, our method offers rapid analysis time (7 min) and exhibits excellent reproducibility with RSD values below 2 %. The sensor's reusability over multiple cycles provides additional economic benefits, while its simple operation using standard fluorescence spectroscopy makes it accessible for routine analysis. Furthermore, the method's robustness across different matrices, including biological fluids and environmental samples, demonstrates its practical utility. The resulting analytical platform offers a balanced combination of sensitivity, selectivity, and simplicity that addresses many limitations of existing methods. The potential for point-of-care testing and minimal instrumentation requirements position this method as a valuable alternative for meropenem monitoring in both clinical and environmental settings.

Despite these advantages, certain limitations of the N-GQDs@MIP sensor should be acknowledged. Matrix effects remain a concern in complex samples, as fluorescence measurements can be affected by colored dissolved organic matter, suspended particles, and other interfering compounds that may cause signal suppression or enhancement. MIP-based sensors can suffer from batch-to-batch variability due to the heterogeneous distribution of binding sites and diffusion limitations, requiring careful quality control during sensor preparation. Template leakage and slow mass transfer can affect sensor performance, particularly in highly complex matrices. The method requires fluorescence spectroscopy instrumentation, which, while more accessible than LC-MS systems, may not be available in all analytical laboratories. Additionally, fluorescence sensors are susceptible to environmental interferences such as temperature changes and pH variations, which may require correction factors for accurate quantification. Besides, the relatively large size of N-GQDs@MIP particles could potentially contribute to light scattering interference during fluorescence measurements. However, the low particle concentration used (7.6 % v/v), large Stokes shift (365/450 nm), and consistent reproducibility (RSD <2 %) demonstrate that such effects are minimal under optimized conditions. Future method development could benefit from particle size optimization to further minimize potential scattering contributions. In conclusion, while the developed N-GQDs@MIP fluorescence sensor presents certain limitations that require consideration during method implementation, the overall analytical performance demonstrates significant improvements over existing techniques in terms of sensitivity, cost-effectiveness, and practical applicability. The balance between advantages and limitations positions this method as a valuable complementary technique to existing analytical approaches, particularly suited for applications where rapid, cost-effective, and sensitive detection of meropenem is required in resource-limited settings or for high-throughput screening purposes.

In summary, a highly sensitive and selective fluorescence sensor for the determination of meropenem based on N-GQDs@MIP was successfully developed and characterized. The sensing mechanism on the molecular level revealed a static quenching process driven by strong host-guest interactions between the meropenem analyte and the imprinted polymer recognition sites as evident from the Stern-Volmer analysis and thermodynamic calculations. Response surface methodology using central composite design optimization enabled the systematic optimization of key experimental parameters, including the pH, time, and the N-GQDs@MIP concentration. A significant quadratic model was obtained with a satisfactory R2 of 0.956 and an adjusted R2 of 0.935, demonstrating the validity and reliability of the proposed methodology. The optimized sensor exhibited excellent analytical performance with a wide linear range of 25-1500 ng/mL, a low LOD of 7.42 ng/mL, and high selectivity against structurally similar antibiotics. Furthermore, the sensor demonstrated good stability and reusability over multiple cycles, highlighting its robustness and cost-effectiveness for practical applications. The sensor was successfully applied to the determination of meropenem in various real-world samples, including pharmaceutical formulation, spiked human serum, urine, and environmental water samples, with satisfactory recoveries and precision, validating its practical utility.

Future research efforts should focus on expanding the applicability of the N-GQDs@MIP sensor to the detection of other clinically relevant antibiotics and exploring its potential for on-site or point-of-care monitoring applications. The sensor's generalizability should be further validated through analysis of diverse sample types, including different categories of wastewater such as industrial effluents, multiple pharmaceutical formulations from various manufacturers, and additional biological matrices such as cerebrospinal fluid, wound exudates, and tissue homogenates. This expanded sample diversity would establish the sensor's performance across a broader range of analytical challenges and matrix complexities. Integrating the sensor with portable instrumentation and optimizing the sample preparation steps could further enhance the sensor's practicality and user-friendliness. Additionally, more detailed investigations into the underlying fluorescence quenching mechanisms and the influence of various interfering species would contribute to a deeper understanding of the sensor's operation and aid in the development of even more selective and robust analytical platforms. Overall, this work demonstrates the potential of N-GQDs@MIP as a versatile and promising analytical tool for the sensitive and selective determination of meropenem, with significant implications for clinical diagnostics, therapeutic drug monitoring, and environmental monitoring applications.
